questions.question: Explain the divide and conquer approach used in Merge Sort with an example for an array of 8 elements.
keyPoints: Divide: Split array into two halves repeatedly until single elements remain
Conquer: Sort each subarray recursively
Combine: Merge sorted subarrays from bottom to top
Example: [38,27,43,3,9,82,10] → divide into [38,27,43,3] and [9,82,10] → further divide → sort and merge back

questions.question: Write the recurrence relation for Merge Sort and solve it using the Master theorem.
keyPoints: Comparing with T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n): a=2, b=2, f(n)=n^1
Since n^(log₂2) = n^1, we have f(n) = Θ(n^1)
Case 2 of Master Theorem: T(n) = Θ(n^1 log n) = Θ(n log n)

questions.question: Why is Merge Sort considered better than Quick Sort for applications requiring guaranteed performance?
keyPoints: Quick Sort has O(n²) worst-case but O(n log n) average case
Merge Sort guarantees O(n log n) for worst, average, and best cases
Merge Sort is stable while Quick Sort is typically unstable
Merge Sort is more predictable for real-time systems

questions.question: What is theMerge operation? Explain how two sorted subarrays are merged with an example.
keyPoints: Merge takes two sorted subarrays and combines them into one sorted array
Use two pointers, one for each subarray
Compare elements, place smaller element in auxiliary array
Continue until all elements are placed
Example: [2,5] and [3,6] → compare 2 and 3 → take 2 → compare 5 and 3 → take 3 → compare 5 and 6 → take 5 → take 6 → result [2,3,5,6]

questions.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of Merge Sort?
keyPoints: Advantages: O(n log n) guaranteed, stable sort, works well for linked lists, parallelizes well
Disadvantages: O(n) auxiliary space required, not in-place, overhead of recursion

questions.question: Trace through Merge Sort for the array: [12, 5, 7, 11] showing all steps.
keyPoints: Divide: [12,5] and [7,11]

questions.question: Prove that Merge Sort requires O(n log n) time complexity.
keyPoints: At each level of recursion tree, we merge n elements costing O(n) time
Height of recursion tree = log₂n (halving n each time)
Total time = n × log₂n = O(n log n)
Alternatively: Using recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n), solving gives O(n log n)

questions.question: Write a complete pseudocode for Merge Sort algorithm and analyze its time and space complexity. Also explain why Merge Sort is preferred for external sorting.
keyPoints: Pseudocode with MERGE-SORT and MERGE functions clearly written
Time complexity: O(n log n) for all cases with proof
Space complexity: O(n) for auxiliary array + O(log n) for recursion stack
External sorting: works well with tape drives and large files that don't fit in memory, sequential access pattern is efficient
Stable sort property important for multi-key sorting

questions.question: Compare Merge Sort with Quick Sort under the following headings: (a) Approach (b) Time complexity (c) Space complexity (d) Stability (e) Worst-case behavior.
keyPoints: (a) Divide and conquer - both use it but Quick Sort partitions in place, Merge Sort divides then merges
(b) Quick Sort: O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst; Merge Sort: O(n log n) all cases
(c) Quick Sort: O(log n) stack space; Merge Sort: O(n) auxiliary space
(d) Quick Sort: unstable; Merge Sort: stable
(e) Quick Sort degrades with sorted input; Merge Sort consistent

questions.question: Derive the recurrence relation for Merge Sort and solve it using recursion tree method to prove the time complexity is Θ(n log n).
keyPoints: Recurrence: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn for n>1, T(1) = d
At level i: 2^i subproblems each of size n/2^i, work per level = 2^i × c(n/2^i) = cn
Number of levels = log₂n
Total T(n) = cn × log₂n + cn = Θ(n log n)
Space analysis: O(n) for auxiliary array
